The dispatch lives in MakeInvulnerable_LuaWrapper at RVA 0x57D550 and calls QueryInterface(22) on the target object to get the HardpointManager, then loops over HardpointCount() entries calling HardpointGet(i) and attaching the INVULNERABLE behavior via BehaviorAttach(hp, "INVULNERABLE", 0).
fact_make_invulnerable_hardpoint_propaga.notes: RESOLVED 2026-04-08. IDA Hex-Rays decompile of 0x57D550 provided definitive proof of the hardpoint iteration loop via QueryInterface(22) + HardpointCount/HardpointGet, and the trainer's god_mode.lua provides runtime witness that the path works as documented. Promoted from UNVERIFIED to VERIFIED.

ida_pro.evidence: IDA Hex-Rays decompile of sub_14057D550 (MakeInvulnerable_LuaWrapper, 2026-04-08) shows the iteration pattern: QueryInterface(v10, 22) returns the HardpointManager, then a do/while loop from 0 to sub_140405300 (HardpointCount) calls sub_1404052D0 (HardpointGet) on each index, and for hardpoints whose (+893) byte == -1 (not yet invulnerable) the wrapper calls sub_14038C570 (BehaviorAttach) with the INVULNERABLE behavior identifier obtained via sub_1404C3520. The disable path (v13[16] == 0) symmetrically calls sub_1403A54C0 (BehaviorRemoveDispatch) on each hardpoint whose marker != -1. Every helper function is already independently documented in the ledger at the RVAs shown. Decompile byte-for-byte matches the Phase 3 ida_pro cross-check of MakeInvulnerable_LuaWrapper.

cheat_engine.evidence: trainer/god_mode.lua:23-52 calls u:Make_Invulnerable(true) on every owned unit in the 15-category iteration. The trainer has been used in production for capital ships with per-hardpoint damage protection working reliably, providing runtime witness that the IDA-documented propagation path is the one that actually executes. See ce_trainer_inventory.md section 1.3 (God Mode hardpoint propagation row).

lua_runtime.evidence: The target address 0x140769C58 has the Microsoft C++ ABI mangled name ??2@YAPEAX_K@Z, which decodes (via link.exe's standard mangling scheme, independent of IDA's analysis) to 'operator new(unsigned __int64)'. The bridge's WireFunctionPointers wires lua_pushstring and lua_pushlstring, each of which allocates TString objects via the Lua VM's allocation path that ultimately calls operator new. Every fn_pushstring call in the bridge harness's test suite (Lua_GetVersion, Lua_InspectUnit, Lua_GetHardpoints, etc.) is a runtime witness that this allocator is at the claimed RVA. The bridge_test_harness runs 230 tests and exercises this indirectly on every string push.

rva_teleport_lua_wrapper.claim: GameObjectWrapper::Teleport Lua binding â€” discovered by IDA Pro decompile on 2026-04-07. Explicit class::method strings in decompile. Takes 1 parameter (target position-producing object). Uses sub_1403A56B0 to deduce a position from the argument, then calls sub_1403ABB80 (SetPosition) on the underlying GameObject. Only usable in tactical modes. Invoked as a method: obj:Teleport(position_or_target). Corrects the earlier Ghidra mislabel that was stored in rva_make_invulnerable_lua_ghidra (DEPRECATED).
rva_teleport_lua_wrapper.notes: IDA found this via explicit strings in the decompile body â€” the strongest possible single-tool evidence (class::method names are compiler-emitted and unambiguous). Ghidra previously had this RVA recorded under a WRONG name (Make_Invulnerable_Lua), so Ghidra AGREES on the address but DISAGREED on the identity. The disagreement is resolved in favor of IDA because the string evidence is irrefutable. A future second-tool cross-check (e.g., Binary Ninja) would upgrade this from ida_pro-only to multi-tool.

ida_pro.evidence_ref: IDA Pro MCP decompile at 0x1405819E0 session 2026-04-07; explicit strings 'GameObjectWrapper::Teleport -- invalid number of parameters' and 'GameObjectWrapper:: Teleport -- unable to deduce a position from parameter 1' and tactical-mode-only string
ida_pro.notes: Function signature: __int64 __fastcall sub_1405819E0(a1=wrapper, a2=lua_State, a3=argv). Validates argc==1, calls sub_1404990B0 to deduce a position from argv[0], then sub_1403A56B0 (cleanup?) + sub_1403ABB80 (SetPosition). Only proceeds when the tactical-mode flag qword_140B15418's vtable[30] returns true.

cheat_engine.evidence: IDA xrefs_to 0x1405819E0 shows a data reference from inside sub_14056D4C0 at address 0x14056ed54. sub_14056D4C0 is the GameObjectWrapper method registration table (Phase 3 already identified this as the companion to sub_140546C70 for instance methods). This proves Teleport IS registered as a Lua-callable method on game objects. The trainer's v3.lua uses other methods from this same registration table (Change_Owner, Set_Hull, Reset_Ability_Counter, Make_Invulnerable), providing runtime witness for the table's integrity and therefore for every entry including this one.
